candidates.stmt:
Victor has lived in Borough since 2014 and has been your local councillor since 2018. Since 2022, he has been the Leader of the Opposition on Southwark Council working hard to get a better deal for residents across the borough. 
During his time as a councillor Victor has led the campaign to save the RV1 bus, fought for better air quality, has represented residents' concerns in the planning system, secured a permanent memorial to the Borough Market and London Bridge attacks.

He is chair and cofounder of Pride in Southwark and helped secure a new LGBTQ+ space at Bankside yards. He is a trained planner and has a professional background delivering affordable housing and new health facilities.

In the Town Hall, Victor has used his position to challenge Labour's poor record on affordable housing and tackling the climate emergency.

Contact Victor: victor.chamberlain@southwark.gov.uk

Hellen is a former journalist now working in digital marketing. Originally from Brazil, Hellen has lived in Borough and Bankside for a number of years. She has been the Vice Chair of Living Bankside since 2024 because she is passionate about our community. Hellen is passionate about improving community safety in Borough and Bankside.

David first moved to the borough in 2006 and has worked locally in SE1 since 2015.

He believes passionately that we have an opportunity to re-shape Southwark for the better after the pandemic. That includes protecting and increasing space for parks and trees, building more affordable housing, and putting local residents at the heart of the Council’s decision-making.

David has worked in the public and charitable sectors on policy development, partnerships and negotiations. He is currently director of communications at a global affairs think tank and a trustee for a charity combating bullying in schools.

I have lived in Borough my whole life and I grew up seeing suffering around me I find unacceptable like homelessness right next to skyscrapers that are held up as signs of progress or how it is now normal for secondary schools to collect food for food-banks. How have we let things fall this far? I have spent my whole life driven by a desire to improve the world and wish to leave it better than I found it. Having the honour of representing Borough & Bankside would allow me to pursue this goal of helping people and making hope normal again.

I have lived in Southwark for 35 years, and after a varied career in education, as a childminder and early years specialist, I now work as an Educational Officer for the London Wildlife Trust. Working with and for families I know how important it is that we ensure that all have the opportunities our great Borough can offer. We need to work hard to make Borough and Bankside a safe and accessible space for all who work and live in this historic ward.

Alastair has lived in London most of his life and over 3 years in Borough. Having finished a sales career and child care responsibilities; he qualified as an Adviser for Citizens Advice in 2012 managing welfare, debt, money and energy issues. He volunteers with Citizens Advice Southwark helping local residents with digital support. Alastair stands for social justice and a fairer, greener borough.

Councillor Dora Dixon-Fyle has been a hard-working Camberwell Green councillor since 1998 and has advocated for thousands of residents. Dora moved to Southwark from Sierra Leone aged 3, attending local schools and then South Bank University. Dora received her MBE from Queen Elizabeth II in 2011 for her services to local government.

Jen Thornton is a campaigner who has worked with neighbours, charities, businesses and unions on local and national campaigns, winning for working people and communities. She has been a union rep, supporting colleagues with issues at work, a school governor, and is on the board of a national charity which makes digital tools to improve access to democracy.

James Moyse moved to Camberwell Green in 2014. He supports his neighbours with housing issues by being involved in his local residents’ association, and has previously worked as a journalist at the BBC and Sky News. James volunteers at 1st Southwark Scouts, giving local young people opportunities to learn and have adventures.

I've lived in Camberwell for nearly 10 years. I work as a teacher and promote Camberwell in my free time. I have been involved in many environmental campaigns, but I'm keen to make real changes to reflect the green transition that many of us want.

Priorities: Southwark Council's Repairs team is currently failing its suffering residents. This must be fixed as a start to then making Southwark look more like it's part of a world city in a wealthy, advanced country. We must use unoccupied buildings as homes. I'll be more transparent with council activity and support expanding our capacity to generate renewable energy, e.g. from food waste and on new council buildings.

I moved to Camberwell when I was 3, from a flat off Walworth Road. I’ve seen local council services deteriorate here. Austerity hasn’t made anything more efficient, it’s just pushed financial burdens onto individuals and families. Diminishing the state's responsibility for necessary services. Taxes go up, and new fees are introduced, while the value and quality of services goes down.

For me, well-being is the number one priority. It’s what politics is about. That means:  
- working with residents on community projects, 
- championing independent local business, 
- fighting government cuts, and 
- reassessing council contracts with private entities to make sure they’re fiscally responsible and working for the public good.

I’ve worked across Southeast London, in hospitality, at libraries, and as a park gardener. I see how much community spaces, nightlife, and natural landscapes mean to people (I also know how many jobs you need to stay afloat these days).

As a councillor I will focus on protecting those spaces from funding cuts. Additionally, surveillance overreach is a priority for me, making reviewing any existing contracts with tech firms, and the rising appliance of facial recognition on our streets other focus issues.

South London born and bred, Councillor Sarah King is leader of Southwark Labour and has represented the people of Champion Hill since 2014. Sarah is passionate about tackling the housing crisis, making our communities safer and leading a council you can trust to get things right, having previously served as Labour’s housing lead for two years.

Bill Williams has over 30 years social work experience, working with London’s homeless for a major central London charity. He is passionate about resolving the housing and the climate crises. Bill is currently the Constituency Office Manager for Neil Coyle MP in Bermondsey & Old Southwark, and previously served as a councillor in Rotherhithe ward (2014-2022).

Michael Millar is a retired NHS hospital doctor, who has lived in Camberwell for 18 years. His support for the Green Party comes from a desire to ensure that the natural world that our children inherit is rich and diverse, and that the potential to live well is not constrained by environmental damage. Cycling is his main mode of transport and he strongly supports further development of the cycling network in London. He continues to support medical research in a variety of ways and has become increasingly concerned about the relationship between inequality and ill health. He favours those social, housing and educational policies which help children develop their full potential, and which reduce inequality of opportunity.

Environmental issues are a strong motivator for me: I work in climate and energy policy, supporting environmental NGOs with technical research and analysis on topics ranging from deforestation to the electric vehicle transition to corporate emissions reporting. But whatever the topic -- and lets face it, there are few non-nuanced topics left out there -- I hope to bring a balanced-but-reasoned approach to framing the discussion and finding solutions.

I've lived in London for much of my life (with prolonged stints in New York and New Delhi). I've never voted anything but Green, and have followed the steady efforts of our Green representatives in Westminster and in councils across the country. The recent surge of enthusiasm and ambition has been pretty astonishing. I mean, we all want to live in a vibrant, healthy, and humane society, and I see the Greens as offering unique clarity on the values that will guide them towards that goal, as well as openness to engage seriously with practical detail (love a bit of detail).

It's glib maybe but I've been so impressed by my fellow Green candidates who are prepared to stand for their values while also fighting for the interests of their communities and constituents. We are uniquely placed to offer an alternative-but-constructive voice on Southwark Council: on safer quieter streets, social and economic inequality, improved air, green space and wellbeing, school amenities, support for those walloped by the cost of living, on the responsiveness of the council infrastructure to issues raised by residents, etc. Check out the Southwark Green Party manifesto here https://www.southwarkgreenparty.org.uk/manifesto, and note there may be items you agree with or disagree with, but the important thing is to have to opportunity for an honest discussion.

A couple more things about me. I have a PhD in physics but numbers still scare me sometimes. I believe volunteering is a wonderful way to push one's comfort zone and cross unexpected pat

Originally from Germany, Irina has been a London resident and civil rights activist for 25 years. From 2019 to 2020, she represented Londoners as a Member of the European Parliament.

Irina lives locally here in Southwark and feels strongly about improving housing, air quality, and access to essential local services.

In her day job, Irina is a lawyer and business consultant specialising in social and environmental sustainability issues. Throughout her career, she has volunteered for multiple human rights charities including Refugees at Home and The Medical Foundation (now Freedom from Torture).

Felix has lived in Elephant and Castle for over a decade. In his career, he has focused on global climate policy with WWF, decarbonisation with Boston Consulting Group, and community impact with HX Expeditions. He will bring his expertise in managing large budgets and teams to ensure that Southwark delivers.

Suzanne has lived in Chaucer for nearly 4 years and London most of her adult life. She has worked in business as a lawyer and sustainability expert. She chairs a London environmental charity and sits on the board of a large Essex based social housing provider. Suzanne is an experienced leader who knows how to get things done in large complex organisations. She campaigns to save London's green spaces, for wealth taxes and a more equal society.

Pascale is a mother, carer, and school librarian who has lived in social housing in Southwark for over 30 years. She has organised to defend local estates through campaigns including  Fill Empty Homes and Yes to Fair Redevelopment. She supports rent controls, decent repairs, SEN provision, disability rights, and full divestment from companies complicit in the genocide in Gaza.

Councillor Joseph Vambe grew up on the Rockingham Estate in Chaucer, the ward he now represents. He is now the Labour lead on young people and community engagement, championing residents’ influence on local decision-making. Joseph was the first ‘Stormzy Scholar’, awarded a fully funded place at the University of Cambridge.

Robert Smeath has lived here for over 30 years. He is a solicitor who has volunteered as a housing lawyer and debt adviser with the Mary Ward Legal Centre which now runs Blackfriars Settlement. Robert is a former school governor and charity trustee, distributing grants to groups supporting elderly people across Southwark.

Saidat Oketunde has spent over 20 years supporting women, young people and families. As Chair of the Southwark Muslim Forum, founder of Faiths Women Community UK and a school governor, she has built local partnerships and supported residents with skills training, food security, mental health support and community safety projects.

I have lived in Southwark for 15 years, attended university in South London and now work here too. I have a strong and long-standing connection to this borough and care deeply about the people who call it home.  I am a passionate Liberal who believes in equality, social justice and making sure everyone feels safe and supported.
I have volunteered with Crisis at Christmas, helping the homeless, and I have worked with former Councillor Poddy Clark on community engagement and casework, listening to residents, tackling local issues and standing up for those who need help.  I have recently joined the campaign to keep the public counter at Walworth Police Station open 24/7, because accessible policing and safer neighbourhoods are essential for all of us. This work has shown me how much stronger Southwark becomes when everyone has a voice. I am standing to fight for safer streets, better housing and a fairer society. I will be visible and represent our community with honesty and hard work.

Tori Griffiths has lived in Dulwich Hill for 5 years and she loves this neighbourhood. Backed by 15 years of working in the voluntary sector, Tori believes in the power of community-led change, and the value of every neighbour’s voice.

William Houngbo is a former councillor who left the Southwark Liberal Democrats to join Labour, citing the lack of community representation amongst the Lib Dems. He has worked on projects including safe spaces for domestic violence survivors and fundraising to protect the environment. William loves our diverse borough and enjoys cycling and running marathons.

Everyday, I look forward to contributing to this ever inclusive, multinational city called London. I receive a great sense of satisfaction working alongside colleagues from other Countries within our treasured NHS where I work as a Nurse. I get a great sense of security and stability from living in the same area where I was born. I am concerned that future generations may not be so lucky. I will always fight for mixed, sustainable communities. These cultural exchanges has enhanced my life and adds immense value to our Nation. I am a Liberal because I believe in a fair, free and open society. I want to make London equal.  I want to make London Liberal.

Councillor Margy Newens has lived in Dulwich Village for over 20 years. She is a school governor and has volunteered locally as an adult literacy tutor and for a group supporting refugees. After roles in international development and trade, she currently works for a social enterprise supporting sustainably minded small businesses.

Councillor Richard Leeming was elected in 2018, serving the needs of the variety of people who live in the ward. Richard’s family have lived in Dulwich since the 1980s. He’s a self-employed innovation consultant working on digital projects for public benefit. Richard is the Labour lead on sports and leisure.
Ed Hill-Smith, your Liberal Democrat candidate for Dulwich Village. I live in Dulwich, raising a family and a keen member of Dulwich Lawn Tennis Club.

Ruth Bannister has lived in Dulwich Wood for almost 20 years, and has long been an active member of the local community. During the pandemic, Ruth managed a local mutual aid group. If elected, she will be a strong advocate for improving housing services, green issues and Dulwich Wood’s schools.

Sean Hannigan has lived in Dulwich since 2022, and previously volunteered at a Southwark advice charity and with the conservationists in Dulwich Wood. Sean has made Southwark his home, and if elected, is determined to promote community empowerment.

I have 20 years’ experience in the social justice sector internationally, advocating for access to education, criminal justice reform, decent work and humane migration and asylum policies. I founded a consultancy practice that provides strategy advice to funders and charities and offers pro bono services to community-based organizations. A leader in the London softball community, I am committed to functional green spaces and inclusive recreation opportunities. Having settled in Dulwich 6 years ago, I am eager to put my passion and policy know-how to work for my neighbours in Southwark.

I have called the southern part of the borough home for 14 years. I am deeply committed to protecting and enhancing local biodiversity, community character, and public spaces.

As a lawyer, I have represented a wide range of people, including tenants, homeless people, asylum seekers, young offenders, and road traffic victims. My work for environmental NGOs ranged from aiding community campaigns to changing national planning and transport policy.

I serve as a trustee of a grant-making foundation and, as a mediator, help neighbours across south London resolve disputes.

A Green Party member for decades, I proposed the party's first ever successful parliamentary amendments, and this year contributed to its response to the Government's devolution agenda.

Lewis has lived in Southwark for several years. He regularly volunteers for a local food poverty charity and believes in giving back to the community. Throughout his career as a parliamentary caseworker he assisted people with a wide variety of issues, from bin collections to housing. He is dedicated to ensuring that the people of Dulwich Wood have a strong voice on the Council, that their views are heard, and that residents have a proactive representative who will roll his sleeves up. If elected, he will work with tenants' associations to ensure fast repairs for tenants, push for tougher police action on phone theft, shoplifting and anti-social behaviour, and regular, reliable bin collections.

Guy has lived in Southwark for 20 years. He has a young family who enjoy spending time in the area's glorious parks and woods. He believes there is nothing more important than the voice of local people and will focus on the issues affecting daily lives in Dulwich Wood. He's committed to being a hands-on, approachable Councillor who listens and delivers results for residents. Guy loves sport and can be found happily cheering on Dulwich Hamlet on a Saturday afternoon. If elected, he will work with Network Rail and TfL to get step-free access at Sydenham Hill Station, and work with local charities to establish a hub to provide support for elderly residents living alone in Dulwich.

As one of the few candidates that actually lives in the ward, I care about local issues.

As a father, I aim to make life safer and easier for all parents in the ward.

As a mathematician, economist and professional risk manager I want to make sure your tax money is spent wisely, fairly and efficiently.

As a liberal democrat, I aim to build and safeguard a fair, free and open society, in which we seek to balance the fundamental values of liberty, equality and community, and in which no one shall be enslaved by poverty, ignorance or conformity. Fair taxes, fairly distributed.

It's time for a change in Southwark; I will not take your vote for granted.

Councillor Mohamed Deen moved to Southwark from Sierra Leone aged 17 and made Walworth his home. Mohamed champions Faraday’s diverse community and has campaigned for improvements to the Aylesbury redevelopment. At work he is a union representative, supporting his colleagues to win a better deal on pay and conditions.

Felicia Johnston is a lifelong Southwark local who grew up in care, fuelling her passion for tackling social inequities. A project manager, she recently spearheaded a major local authority programme to drive up standards for private renters. She has also served as a school governor for six years, championing inclusion for pupils with SEND.

Sasjkia Otto moved to London aged 18, lived in a hostel, and walked between shops asking for work.

After a private sector career, she turned to Westminster to right the wrongs she’s encountered on her journey.

She’s helped develop government policy, change the law and secure investment to fix public services, build homes, and make life better for workers, disabled people and pensioners.

I was born and raised in South London and now live on the Brandon estate in Southwark. My day job is in careers and skills development, and I volunteer as a teaching assistant. I see the anxiety our children and young people have about the future, and in Southwark we have a real chance to give the next generation a borough with cleaner air, better opportunities, and secure, affordable housing.

Suzy has lived in Southwark for over twenty years and raised her family here. She loves our diverse neighbourhoods, home to old-timers and newcomers alike — that is Southwark's unique strength, worth celebrating and fighting for.

She supports well-built and cared-for social housing that genuinely serves local residents. Safer streets for children. Cleaner pavements and vibrant green spaces. Opportunities for young people. She aims to be an effective and transparent communicator between council and residents — for a greener, better-run Southwark that delivers what people deserve and are paying for.

Although I was born in Mexico and I am a proud immigrant, I have called London, especially South East London, home for the past 9 years. I want to give back to Southwark what it has given me: a home, culture, and an overall good experience. My main goal is to serve Faraday residents by understanding their experiences and listening to their feedback to identify their specific needs and concerns. I understand it will be a challenge, but I do not have any ulterior motives besides preserving the good things Faraday has and addressing its issues.

Rebecca has lived in Camberwell with her family for over 15 years. She works in fundraising for an international development charity and is also a Trustee of a charitable trust managing her local park (just across the border in Lambeth).

Her priorities are providing safe and green spaces for children and young people in the area, as well as more opportunities for young people to access employment and training. She is keen on helping improve local parks and green spaces given their vital importance to everyone's health and wellbeing, and in particular also ensuring that all communities in our area have equal access to them.

Eloise Waldon-Day is a long-time passionate local campaigner. She helped push Southwark Council to declare a climate emergency, shaped their climate plans, and founded the Southwark Climate Justice Coalition, uniting 15+ local groups to drive community climate action. Eloise is running to be a councillor as an extension of her community action - seeking to bring these voices, and the urgency of equitable climate action, to the council.

Professionally, Eloise is Head of Strategic Partnerships at C40 Cities, a global network of mayors united in tackling the climate crisis, and brings global climate solutions back to Southwark. Her career has spanned supporting women's leadership in Rwanda, and advancing sexual and reproductive health access worldwide with MSI and ActionAid.

Eloise can be found with her two dogs on Peckham Rye, playing sport, and volunteering at her local youth club.

James has been the councillor for Goose Green, in East Dulwich, since 2018. He is hardworking and responsive, with one of the best records on casework in the whole borough. Until recently, he was the Cabinet member for Clean Air, Streets and Waste and under his leadership, Southwark had the highest recycling rate in inner London. He is a primary school teacher, trade unionist and socialist.

George loves East Dulwich where he has lived for 45 years. He was an active member of the Labour party all his adult life - until it became clear that Labour no longer stood for progress – and he joined the Green Party in 2023.

George is a member of the UVW trade union and is particularly passionate to promote the ideals of good quality affordable housing for all and solidarity with Palestine - and to play a part in defeating the politics of division and hate being promoted by Reform and Labour.

George retired recently from a lifetime working in social housing, mostly in Southwark. He volunteers on the residents’ board running a Southwark Council estate and was a school governor at Heber Primary School for 18 years. His son attended Heber and Charter schools.

I’ve lived in South London for 30 years and have two teenage children. In my day job I manage large telecom contracts.

I have volunteered for wildlife and road safety projects, and chaired Southwark Cyclists for many years. I have been a special needs school governor. I am motivated by injustice. I want to leave the planet a better place.

I am proud of achievements as a Liberal Democrat Councillor since 2006 from the new cinema to the revamped leisure centre. I have made new primary schools happen and won battles with the Labour Council for a new secondary school.

I want to make my London safer. I want to make my London Liberal.

Cllr Portia Mwangangye says, “I may not be Southwark Born but I am definitely Southwark Bred”. From arriving as an asylum seeker to growing up across our local estates, this community has shaped her. Portia’s legal and community work led her to become a councillor, driven to serve Southwark and ensure our young people have every opportunity to thrive.

Liam McGrath is a transport and housing expert, with experience in community-led development. He has built award-winning council homes through Leather market Community Benefit Society, founded SE1 Solar to cut energy bills, and leads on transport analysis at TfL.  He hopes to bring practical skills in housing, transport, and grassroots engagement to Goose Green.

Councillor Sam Dalton has been a councillor for London Bridge and West Bermondsey since 2022. He has led big improvements in the ward over that time, including on housing, such as securing new council homes, a pioneering affordable, key worker housing development, and launching a major investigation on behalf of residents on a big estate, into major works issues.

Cllr Dalton has also led major security improvements across estates in the ward, funded the upgrade of Alfred Salter playground, Downside Fisher Youth Centre, and Magdalen Community Hall, and championed Southwark's "Streets for People" strategy by making walking and cycling easier.

He brought a motion to Southwark Council calling for voting rights to be extended for migrants, which received unanimous support, and has led the development of Southwark's first ever Older People's Housing Strategy as Deputy Cabinet Member for Supported Housing.

In his day job, Sam works on policy and campaigns for a homelessness charity.

I am an experienced local Councillor who has helped thousands of residents over 16 years. I have improved and created green spaces, protected communities and heritage from inappropriate development, and opposed the Garden Bridge

As a gay man I am passionate about equal rights. I have been a London Lighthouse volunteer and done many runs for charity including the London Marathon. For my daytime job I work in local government as a community safety officer.

I am motivated by human rights and protecting the environment, I am a committed European.

I want to make my London more equal. I want to make my London Liberal.

Selena Gray has lived in Southwark all her life and has lived in London Bridge and West Bermondsey for over 10 years. She is a Community Champion, helping to improve the neighbourhood for everyone, and a social researcher with over 10 years’ experience studying social inequalities.

Rugiatu Kanu has a deep passion for community service. She advocates against hygiene poverty, and has restored dignity to over 2,000 women in Southwark. Rugiatu currently serves as a Service Manager in a residential home where she oversees the wellbeing and support of vulnerable adults.

Councillor John Batteson was elected in 2022 and is currently the labour lead for climate emergency, jobs and business. He has led the delivery of Southwark’s climate strategy, ranked as 3rd best in the country. John has overseen work done to increase fair pay for local people, resulting in a pay raise for over 7,000 people.

Elected in 2022, Councillor Natasha Ennin has been a tireless champion for the residents she represents. As the Labour lead for community safety, Natasha is a leading voice in the mission to end male violence against women and girls. Her goal is simple: making our neighbourhoods places where people feel proud and safe to live.

I have lived in Southwark for 16 years and am increasingly concerned about the growing impact of wealth inequality. It affects every level of our society and is eroding our democracy. I am an advocate for a fairer society that meets the needs of ordinary people and families. As a former social entrepreneur I have strong community-led values and would love to bring this energy to the work of councillor on behalf of people in Newington.

I first moved to Southwark in 2008 and love our diverse community. Until 2016, I worked in the finance industry and saw firsthand how the system has prioritised profits over people. Since leaving the industry, my research and campaigning has pushed for a fairer financial system that puts climate, living wages, and community health at its core. These are the same issues that I will work on as a councillor.

I am a retired psychologist and have lived around Elephant and Castle for the past 10 years, after moving to the UK from Sweden. I care deeply about the right to decent housing, the cost of living and ensuring residents feel heard. I am committed to fair and equal opportunities for everyone in Newington. I am standing to represent our area with empathy, integrity, and a strong commitment to community

Hello, I'm Rachel Bentley and I am standing again to represent the fantastic ward of North Bermondsey in Southwark.   I’ve lived here for well over a decade now and been proud to represent the area as a local Liberal Democrat councillor for the past four years. I chose this area as my home because I love its wonderful combination of history, vibrancy and culture. Since settling in Bermondsey, I’ve actively participated in various charitable projects. I’ve mentored young people, taught financial literacy and helped deliver food during the Covid pandemic. I am a trustee of a charity that supports young people in Southwark to achieve their educational goals, and I provide emergency shelter to young people who are homeless.   I decided to enter politics to combat the incompetence and scandals which have plagued Westminster. I am committed to restoring trust and integrity in public life.    I spent 16 years overseas, living and working in Japan, the USA and South Africa. In my professional work, I’ve advised governments, families, businesses and charities on many matters, including job creation and land use. In May 2022, I was elected as a councillor for our borough and am the Deputy Leader of the Southwark Liberal Democrat Opposition Council Group.  I stood in the 2024 General Election and came second - next time I plan to win! I am an experienced campaigner and believe we must prioritise safe, affordable and secure housing for all. I’ve campaigned on other issues including support for small businesses, period poverty and electoral reform. I’ve brought together groups from multiple London boroughs against the disruptive Oceandiva party boat and championed the environment by planting trees, campaigning against pollution in the Thames and tackling the blight of litter and fly-tipping.   I’m passionate about the environment and at my happiest walking along the river or through cherished green spaces. I can be found pretty much every Saturday morning at the Southwark Parkru

I have lived in Bermondsey Spa for over ten years now and have been an active member of the community throughout this time, from delivering food during the Covid pandemic, to running the Bermondsey Spa Residents Association for four years, and now managing my 120-unit block.

I love Bermondsey - its many green areas, quiet walks, diverse culture, wonderful sites and amazing food options. But from personal experience as well as from talking to many residents, I am acutely aware of the severe housing problems that far too many people face, no matter whether they are Council tenants or private leaseholders. Improving housing conditions for the residents of Bermondsey by holding landlords and developers accountable for their actions, is the main reason why I decided to stand for councillor with the Liberal Democrats.

On a more personal note, I pursue many hobbies, from cycling to running, chess and photography amongst others, and am a big nature and animal lover.

Patrickson has lived in Bermondsey for the past 24 years.

He is an active member of the local community, having been a governor of St Joseph’s primary school for eight years. He is also involved in his Church, St Gertrude’s, where he sits on the Church council and has been a part of it’s charity fundraising drives.

Patrickson runs his own security business here in Bermondsey and cares about reducing crime in our area. He also wants to see the council reach out more to young people.

Born in London, I moved back in 1979 and have lived in Southwark since 2000. A biologist and film-maker, community science projects and adult literacy coaching keep me busy in retirement. The climate and nature emergencies have such strong links with social justice that the Green Party is my natural home.

Carys Allsopp settled in Southwark in 2019 for her work as a product specialist developing solutions to the climate crisis. She’s a passionate football fan and player, who cares deeply about inclusion in sports and leisure. Carys is keen to use her professional skills in user experience to improve access to council services.

Andy Bruce moved to North Bermondsey in 1994 to become a youth worker at the Salmon Youth Centre and now teaches at a local state secondary school. Andy is currently Chair of the Haddonhall Tenants’ Management Organisation. Previously, he worked for the United Nations on human rights and conflict prevention.

Robin Pollard settled on North Bermondsey’s Dickens Estate in 2021. As a new parent, Robin understands the real pressures facing local families. With 15 years’ experience in fraud investigation and data analytics, he works to tackle crime every day. If elected, he will champion affordable homes and community safety.

Rebecca is a North Walworth resident, born locally, who has returned home after decades of service internationally to serve her community.

She is a human rights and protection specialist with nearly two decades of local and international experience working with children and vulnerable adults, and communities affected by violence, conflict and disaster — including a decade of service with United Nations Peace Operations protecting children in situations of armed conflict. Back in the UK, she has worked in crisis support organisations and as Lead Advocate for young people in Southwark with Barnardo’s. She is also a local artist and a volunteer gardener, and founder of an organisation working with violence-affected young people.

Rebecca is proud of the strong, diverse community in Walworth, but she knows people have been let down too many times. She lives in social housing and sees the impact on young people of growing up amid partially demolished housing and neglect by the local authority. Rebecca believes in tackling the causes of insecurity — housing, jobs and support services — and prioritising needs of local people, not developers. Following her overseas service, she lives with disability, and understands the indignity and delays many people face accessing even basic support.

Rebecca brings professional experience and a real commitment to listen and work hard for her community.

Vanessa is a long time Southwark resident, and passionate advocate for local business, culture and safe communities. She has spent her career building creative spaces and fighting violence against women and girls; from founding a feminist music organisation, and delivering key evidence in the Women's and Equalities committee. With a law degree and a background in alternative dispute resolution and homelessness advocacy, she knows how to fight for people fairly. She believes every neighbourhood should be accessible, thriving, and locally owned.

Dr Saba Manzoor is a Labour candidate rooted in North Walworth, with a British Pakistani background. An environmentalist and community organiser, she has already helped with casework on housing and benefits in the community. Saba is committed to genuinely affordable homes, thriving local high streets, cleaner air and resident-led regeneration.

Philip Wheeler is a full-time firefighter, working to serve and protect the community. He leads trade union organising, and has studied public policy at postgraduate level. Phil combines practical experience with evidence-based decision-making, focused on strong public services, clear communication and making limited resources go further.

Zaid Moosa works in manufacturing innovation and wants to drive Southwark council to evolve and continually improve, ensuring it delivers for everyone but especially those most in need. Zaid also wants to play his part to build a future for Southwark that has exciting opportunities and is future focused.

- It is now time to get a conservative and unionist counsellor in North Walworth, for many decades north Walworth is lack of proper leadership Labour Counsellors have let us down far too long. We now need a change of representation to make a difference and back to basics.

I have been a local resident in Camberwell Green for nearly 10 years and am passionate about serving the local community. My main interests are in community groups, building networks and ensuring fair access to green spaces. I work for a charity supporting refugees in South London and would be excited to represent local residents! I am delighted to be standing as your Green candidate for Nunhead and Queens Road. We Greens demand real change for the people we represent. Having Greens in any room makes the other parties lift their game and work smarter and harder for their communities. Greens work hard for their constituents and we aren't whipped so can really represent the views of residents. Voting Green is never a wasted vote, more crosses in the ballot box shows that Green policies are important and supported!

Claire is a Peckham-based community activist, small business owner, cancer survivor and mum who has lived in the area for 25 years. In 2020 Claire co-founded Nunhead Knocks community mutual aid group, providing and co-ordinating practical support in SE15 through Covid. In winter 2022/23 the group pivoted to providing small community grants, fundraising over £40k over three years. In one tough winter alone almost £20k in individual grants of £50 meeting the basic needs of food and fuel. Claire came to politics later in life following major life experiences: new motherhood, cancer treatment and applying for Universal Credit. She vowed that once recovered, she would make a positive contribution to her community and do all she could to leave the world a better place for her son. She has campaigned in 8 elections in the last 8 years, and as Green Party Candidate for Lambeth and Southwark in the London Assembly elections has beaten Tories into 3rd place twice and got 20% of the GE vote in Peckham in 2025. Claire is a proven hard-working, caring and respected campaigner who will work her socks off for her local ward if enough people are bold and vote with their heart. The Tories and Reform cannot win here but if enough people "Say YEP for Shep" we could turn Southwark Green in 2026.
I want to be a voice for the people in my area, bring the greens to the table to hold Southwark Council accountable and have a genuine influence on local green policies.

Councillor Gavin Edwards moved to London 26 years ago and has been a Nunhead resident since 2008, where he is raising his children. He works for UNISON, the UK’s biggest trade union, as the social care lead, representing care workers. Elected in 2010, Gavin is a dedicated and active representative.

Cllr Sandra Rhule has lived and worked in Southwark all her life. She is an energetic advocate, who speaks up for the most vulnerable in our communities. Driven by her lived experiences, Sandra’s key focus is tackling the housing crisis. She enjoys doing casework and speaking up for those who need it.

Marq Bailey is a local postie, trade unionist and parent, and has built an international career in film and photography. He is committed to environmental stewardship, community empowerment and delivering lasting progress for residents across Southwark today and for future generations in every local neighbourhood.

Luisa is a Liberal Democrat councillor in Camden, having been successfully elected in her first ever election in May 2018. She joined the party after the 2016 referendum. She has since campaigned passionately for the UK to retain its EU membership. In her first year as a councillor, her motion in favour of a People’s Vote was passed by Camden Council.

Luisa is a fluent French speaker and proud Londoner. A former journalist, she has experience working in both the public and private sectors. She currently manages the UK arm of a French financial communications agency and teaches economic history for a university.

Councillor Evelyn Akoto grew up in Southwark and is now the Labour lead on adult health and social care. She brings experience from health, policy and political work beyond local government, and is driven by a simple aim, to make public services work better for the people who rely on them most.

Councillor Richard Livingstone has represented his local area for 20 years and now works with Peckham’s Member of Parliament to secure better outcomes for local people. Richard has focused on pushing for more and better quality social housing, opening more community spaces, and making Southwark safer.

I have lived in Southwark all of my adult life, I love my patch, and I want to give something back to the area that has let me grow and supported me.

We deserve better. Better services by the council, better maintenance of social housing, better planning and licensing, better value for the money we all pay. We need accountable and effective leadership, and I will stand for integrity, social justice and pragmatism.

Funding from Westminster has been reduced in real terms over the past decade and more, but we need to make sure that the resources we do have are spent in the most effective way possible, and targeted towards those who need them most. We can't have glass high rises next to homelessness, luxury flats with no affordable housing, we need to do better.

Councillor Michael Situ was elected in 2010. During this time, he steered through a new Domestic Abuse Strategy, focused on measures to tackle antisocial behaviour and fought for new council homes. Michael is also focused on advocating for improved services for young people and the revitalisation of our high streets.

Chris has spent the last 10 years advocating for children's rights and now campaigns for the protection of the environment. He currently is a public campaigner for the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWF) where he regularly works together with community groups at the local level to help raise awareness of the importance of nature. He has lived in Southwark for 5 years and is excited to have the opportunity to make a positive difference in people's lives.

Emily made Bermondsey her home over three years ago, and now lives in the Pynfolds Estate as a private renter.

She joined the Liberal Democrats after a talk from Simon Hughes at her sixth form and was elected as a town councillor in her hometown in Sussex. Whilst she served as a councillor, she helped fight Conservative plans to close a local leisure centre.

Emily now works for a major London housing association, consulting residents on estate regeneration.

Councillor Leona Sabina Emmanuel is a Community Champion, and is the current Deputy Mayor of Southwark. Sabina is committed to Peckham, and has decades of experience supporting local people through as a tenants’ association and trade union representative.

I'm standing to be your representative because Labour have failed to make the changes we need or hold power to account - over many decades in control of Councils across England, and now again in Government.

Having spent my life working for change on economic and environmental issues, I realised we need radical change within Parliament and locally in our Councils.

If elected I promise to take strong action on:

air pollution that afflicts all of us, including south London hotspots
    providing truly affordable housing for South Londoners
    protecting our public healthcare system from privatisation and profiteering
    fighting the closure of cultural spaces and small businesses through large-scale    developments

I'm keen that as the world's 6th richest country, all of us in the UK must share in a fairer and more sustainable future.

The Green Party are the main challengers to Labour and Conservatives across London, and the only party effectively challenging the change the agenda.

I have lived in South London for much of my life. Despite leaving school and home aged 13, I studied Environmental Science at University, whilst being involved in environmental research, LGBT activism, and campaigns to protect wildlife habitat. I have established several businesses and housing co-operatives, and around the Iraq war; I help found a protest which prevented building over Crystal Palace Park.

I have the determination, skills, and willpower to ensure we and future generations can benefit from a tolerant and resilient society.

I want to bring to parliament the voice of sustainability and the prospect of sharing our wealth and resources between all, rather than the 1%,

Even a small number of Green MPs can make a world of difference to protect our wages, health service and clean air. So vote for what you believe in.

Councillor Barrie Hargrove has been a Southwark Councillor for over 30 years and is a proud representative. He has held many positions during that time, including Mayor of Southwark. Barrie has lived in Peckham all his life and feels privileged to be able to help local people with problems and complaints.

Councillor Cleo Soanes is a teacher specialising in Special Educational Needs and works tirelessly to seek justice and support for young people with SEND. Cleo takes pride in fighting for the best outcome for Peckham’s people and working to improve the built environment.

Philomena is the mum of two lovely girls. Philomena run's her own business in skincare and studies human resources and business management. Philomena has experience mentoring, coaching and empowering women as well as working with small businesses on training and development.

Councillor Victoria Mills was elected in 2010 and has lived in Peckham Rye for nearly 20 years. She is a secondary school teacher, and her children attend local schools. Vikki’s priorities are improving schools, early years and youth services, local sport and protecting and improving our brilliant green spaces and playgrounds.

Councillor Renata Hamvas has lived in Nunhead for 27 years and was elected in 2010. She campaigns for improvements to transport, community facilities, education, and local green spaces. Renata has supported local sports and youth facilities, and work to revamp local playgrounds. She is also a governor at Kings College Hospital.

Francisca is a researcher working for a social impact organisation, supporting charities and public services to improve how they address social problems and deliver change. Working directly with communities in their local area, she focuses on understanding their needs and making sure that their voices are represented in decision-making. She is well-versed in working with and alongside a wide variety of people. Fran also has over four years of experience delivering at-home care to children and adults. Shaped by her experiences and led by her empathy, she is committed to making sure people feel included in the processes and decisions that affect them, building trust and actively listening to residents to improve their experiences in Southwark.

Steve is a local business owner, running his Sports Massage company on Rye Lane for over 10 years. He’s committed to improving the health and wellbeing of the community, along with promoting biodiversity and access to nature. He is a long term Peckham resident and his son attends Ivydale school.

Colin Boyle has lived in Rotherhithe since 2002, and as soon as he moved here he knew that it was where he wanted to settle and bring up a family. A qualified accountant, Colin leads the delivery of complex IT programmes for the public sector, and this knowledge and experience will enable him to hold Southwark Council to account. “London is changing fast, and the vast development planned for Canada Water will shape our area forever. I absolutely support redevelopment, provided it is the right scale and mix to deliver jobs and a sustainable community, and is supported by the right infrastructure. I believe development should be led by the community, and that Green councillors will be strong, local advocates that Rotherhithe needs at this crucial time.

Councillor Stephanie Cryan’s politics were shaped by growing up on a South London council estate. She was elected as a Labour councillor for Rotherhithe in 2014 and considers it a privilege to serve local residents. Stephanie is the Labour lead on finance, making sure the council remains financially robust.

I have lived in and loved Rotherhithe for more than a decade. I am passionate about addressing the affordability crisis and fighting for real change, not small compromises. We need a local government that listens to and respects residents' concerns, and that's exactly what I intend to deliver if elected.

Councillor Bethan Roberts is a Rotherhithe local who brings 30 years of experience as a local campaigner. First elected in 2022 she has helped residents with issues like housing repairs, improving the local environment and securing funding for local community groups. Bethan is passionate about supporting residents and believes everyone deserves a safe, secure and well-maintained home. Bethan also campaigns for strong public services and a fairer, more inclusive borough, pushing for improvements to the area and working to protect the things that make Rotherhithe a great place to live.

Dear neighbours,

I’m standing for election because I believe our community deserves a council that listens, includes, and delivers. Every day - whether I’m teaching, supporting young people, or working alongside volunteers - I see the strength, kindness, and determination that make this city and area special. You deserve representatives who match that energy.

My promise is simple: I will work hard for you, stay accessible, and make sure your voice is heard in every decision that affects our streets, our homes, and our future. I’m committed to practical solutions, honest conversations, and a council that puts people first.

This election is your chance to choose the kind of representation you want for Rotherhithe. I’m asking for your support so we can build a fairer, safer, more connected community together.

Thank you for taking the time to engage, to ask questions, and to care about the place we all call home. I’d be honoured to represent you.

Warmly,
Sorcha

Vote for Barry Duckett Your local Independent candidate for Rotherhithe on may 7th 26
VOTE BARRY DUCKETT
Independent Candidate for Rotherhithe Ward Thursday, 7th May 2026
For over 50 years, I have been on the frontline fighting for the rights of tenants and residents- long before many of the current councillors were even born. I am standing as an independent because the big parties have failed our community.
Where are the 11,000 new homes promised to you by this Labour-run council when they first came into power three terms ago? Labour has let you down. Ask yourself one question: When did you last actually see your local Ward Councillor? They are knocking on your doors now because they want your vote, but where have they been for the last four years? It is time for a representative who actually listens and delivers. Labour has let you down at every opportunity.

* End the Gridlock: We must t

Councillor Jasmine Ali has energetically represented Rye Lane for 12 years and is Southwark Labour’s Deputy Leader and lead for children, young people and refugees. She works hard to deliver for children, families & refugees, including groundbreaking work to tackle school exclusions, free support for children with mental health needs and delivering Southwark’s Borough of Sanctuary status. Jasmine lives in Rye Lane with her family and their dog, Smokey.

Councillor Esmé Dobson is a full-time carer for her husband who has young onset Alzheimer’s disease. She was elected Labour Councillor for Rye Lane in 2022 and is passionate about wanting to make improvements in social care and on disability issues. Previously, Esmé worked as a translator at the European Commission.

I’m standing as a council candidate for the Green Party and have lived on Rye Lane for over a decade.

Living with hidden disabilities has given me first-hand experience of how policies affecting support like Personal Independence Payment impact people’s lives. I’ve worked on zero-hours contracts building my career in security, so I understand the pressures of insecure work within the cost-of-living crisis.

I’m also a musician involved in community venues and grassroots spaces, and I’ve seen too many close locally. I want to stand up for residents, protect local culture, and build a greener, fairer Peckham where everyone can thrive.

Councillor David Parton has had the privilege of serving the Rye Lane community as a councillor since 2024. David entered politics to fight the climate crisis and tackle urgent social injustices, including poverty, poor-quality housing, and unequal access to opportunities. It would be an honour for him to be re-elected.

I joined the Liberal Democrats because of their strong Remain position.

I have spent ten years working as a fundraiser and campaigner in international development. I am passionate about the environment.

In 20 years living here, I have seen many changes. I believe that, whilst vibrancy continues to develop, the existing community’s needs must be met, particularly housing. I am troubled that the Labour run Council is cosying up to developers instead of securing homes people can afford.

I want a London we can be proud of - from pavements to green spaces. I want a Liberal London.

Your Voice for Rye Lane

I've had the privilege of calling Peckham my home for over 21 years, a place where the vibrant energy and entrepreneurial spirit are truly inspiring. My roots run deep here—my mother was from Nunhead, and I've lived in this ward since 2007. I am passionate about our community and believe in its boundless potential.

Why Vote for Me on July 4?

Supporting Local Businesses:

Our local businesses are the heartbeat of Rye Lane Ward. I am dedicated to championing their growth and success. From the corner shops to the bustling markets, I will work tirelessly to create opportunities and remove barriers, helping our local enterprises thrive.

Improving Transport Links:

Reliable and accessible transport is crucial for our community. I will advocate for enhanced transport connections that make it easier for everyone to get to work, school, and enjoy all that our area has to offer. Whether it’s pushing for better bus routes or supporting sustainable transport options, I am committed to improving our infrastructure.

Ensuring Safer Streets:

Safety is a fundamental right for all residents. As a father of two teenagers, I am acutely aware of the dangers our young people face, including the threat of knife crime. I will actively campaign for initiatives that make our streets safer. This includes advocating for better street lighting, enhancing community policing efforts, and implementing traffic calming measures. My goal is to make Rye Lane a place where everyone—especially our children—feels secure and welcome.

Join Me in Making a Difference

Your vote on July 4 is not just a vote for a candidate, but a vote for a vision—a vision of a thriving, connected, and safe Rye Lane Ward. Let’s work together to harness the potential of our incredible community and build a brighter future for all.

Fatmata Bah is a nurse and an academic. She lives in a council home in South Bermondsey and is currently working towards her PhD. Fatmata is proud to volunteer with Migrant Democracy Leaders and Thorowgood Community Group.

Andy Bates has worked in housing and communities for 40 years, most recently as Chief Executive of Leathermarket JMB, a tenant managed housing organisation. He’s active in community groups including as a trustee of St Olaves charity, secretary of the Thorowgood Community Group and the Labour Housing Group executive committee.

Councillor Sunny Lambe is the current Mayor of Southwark, and was elected in 2014. He has served on local TRA boards, and was chairman of the Southwark Black History Month and Black Heritage Organisation.  He has founded multiple local initiatives, including the Peckham Supplementary School and Southwark Youth Enterprise Project.

Gordon Mackay has lived in Southwark for seven years and is standing to serve as a Ward Councillor in South Bermondsey. Originally from Glasgow and raised in Johannesburg, he has served as a Member of Parliament, Shadow Energy Minister, and Ward Councillor. Prior to life in public service, Gordon was a humanitarian with United Nations Children’s Fund. He has deep experience fighting for residents’ rights, taking on arbitrary administrations to ensure people are seen, heard and prioritised. He will bring this fighting spirit to representing the good people of South Bermondsey.

Since moving here I’ve notice a few ways we could improve how the council operates.

New residents parking permits are hard to get in good time bc data is cross referenced with Land Registry data which takes months to update. I’d like to encourage the council to verify residency through means that don’t include waiting for Land Registry to speed up the process.

Bins in communal areas are often used as fly tipping sites which builds up quickly so the council uses un-allocated budget to remove it which reinforces the behaviour. I’d like to discuss ways to incorporate the service in the budget.

Paths and pavements are incredibly uneven. I’ve noticed every time people visit me here, they trip or stumble at some point! It’s time to smooth out the trip hazards in South Bermondsey so I’ll work on ways to manages this process economically.

Graham has represented Saint George’s Ward since 2018 and lived at the Elephant since the 1980's. 
He is interested in sustainabilty, and advocating for local people.

Lina Usma, originally from Colombia, has been in the borough of Southwark for nearly 20 years. She is the director of Extra Media, a company based in Manor Place. Lina has been involved for many years with the local communities, as she was a Community Development Support Officer at CAS where she had the opportunity to be in touch with many local organisations. As a local tenant and a member of the Latin American Community, Lina is fighting to make sure that community businesses are protected and are not displaced. She is also a trustee in Su Mano Amiga, aiming to help victims of Domestic Violence in Southwark and Lambeth. She is keen to support any initiatives related to women, sustainability and community development as she knows women have so much to offer.

Diana Chichande is a naturalised British citizen, born in Ecuador and raised in Spain, now a parent living in London. Guided by faith and community values, they are proud to serve St George’s ward with care and empathy, tackling housing and cost-of-living challenges while supporting recognition of the Latin community.

Tom Heys has lived locally for nearly 20 years. He is a local housing association board member, a trustee of the Bankside Open Spaces Trust, and helps his follow residents with housing issues. In his professional life, he advocates for equal pay and greater social mobility.

I have made the South East of London my home, after moving to the city nearly five years ago. I have studied in Deptford, marched in Lewisham, and have lived across a number of boroughs in the region. Lewisham and Dulwich are two areas full of life and community, and I am tired of seeing this being eroded by Westminster politics. There are many fundamental issues in this country, making life extremely hard for many people, and I want to offer an alternative for people to vote for against the current lacklustre main parties. I will always stand for economic, social, and climate justice for anyone who calls the constituency their home.

Councillor Ellie Cumbo has lived in Southwark for 15 years, and was elected in 2022. She is proud to be the Labour lead on parks and culture, ensuring that Southwark’s libraries, award-winning green spaces and local arts sector continue to thrive. Professionally, Ellie has a legal background, and has worked on tackling violence against women and girls.

Councillor Ian Wingfield was elected in 1989 and has worked tirelessly to represent local concerns to the Council and other bodies on issues such as housing, the environment, education, planning, welfare and more. Ian is passionate about conserving Camberwell’s character, and keen to tackle local issues such as graffiti, fly-tipping and antisocial behaviour.

Born and raised in Southwark, Councillor Jason Ochere understands first-hand the importance of improving access to opportunities, especially for young people from disadvantaged backgrounds. Elected in 2018, Jason has championed local issues relating to business, jobs, planning, youth violence, the environment and scrutiny of council decisions. He works as a business communications specialist.

Having moved to Southwark some 25 years ago Poddy loves the vibrancy and diversity of the area and feels she has returned to her roots, her Grandfather having been born on the Old Kent Road.

During her career in finance jobs in both the private and public sector, including working on housing contracts, she felt the experience helpful when she served a term as a local councillor.

Now retired Poddy is still involved with Liberal Democrat campaigning and is proud of the way our party aims to listen to and involve the ideas and ambitions of local residents.

Adam has lived here in Surrey Docks for six years. He is disappointed with the Labour council administration ignoring the community here in Surrey Docks when it comes to consulting residents on local developments including the Canada Water Masterplan.

Adam is concerned about housing, planning and parking in Surrey Docks and wants to see the council do a better job of listening and engaging with local residents.

Jane has lived in London for nearly 40 years, over 20 of them in the borough where she was first elected as a Councillor in 2004. She has also been an active member of the resident’s association and School Governor.

She is a member of several litter picking groups around the borough and is passionate about keeping our area tidy. Jane wants to ensure development money invested in Southwark is spent on improving our local area including investing in the Surrey Docks farm and other local projects.

Francesca has lived in Southwark for 18 years and loves the diversity and history of this area. She is a proud first generation immigrant whose parents emigrated from Ireland at a time of ‘no dogs, no blacks, no Irish’ so has an understanding of how difficult it can be to live in a hostile environment. Francesca believes in inclusion and equality for all and that hope and togetherness must rise against the hate and division which is destroying this country. Decent, affordable housing for young people is an urgent priority.

I’m from Bermondsey, Uni aside I have always lived in Southwark and now live in Rotherhithe. Currently training to be a teacher.

Vote Green to send a message to those in Westminster, vote Green to send a message to the system. I was previously Labour. I left once I saw the party was no longer democratic and no longer in any way for the people. Politically homeless for a while I’ve been a Green for over three years.

I will fight for working people on all the key issues such as housing, social care and transport.

As a person that believes and practices personal fitness I want to see our outdoor gyms upgraded to the point where they are exciting to use particularly for young men. The current gyms are largely unchallenging. I want to see more of them across the ward and the borough. I think they provide space for people of all different backgrounds and ethnicities to come together, thus providing a community anchor against the division tactics of the far-right.

Moya O’Rourke believes Surrey Docks deserves the best. If elected, she will continue to fight for the community and its diverse nightlife, art, and theatre. By championing genuinely affordable homes, introducing a new youth guarantee, and creating a safer neighbourhood, she will ensure that everyone in Surrey Docks can reach their full potential.

Henry Bottomley has lived in Surrey Docks and Rotherhithe for 29 years, he previously served as a councillor in Lambeth. As a civil servant he worked on policy areas such as broadband access, work incentives, and lifting people out of poverty. Now retired, Henry volunteers with almshouse residents in Southwark and Greenwich and is a mentor for young people.

Adam Smith is a Barrister and long-standing Surrey Docks resident. Motivated by public service and local pride, Adam’s experience as an advocate means he is well placed to represent the best interests of local people. He is committed to promoting community safety, a fair economy and delivering practical improvements for Surrey Docks residents.

I am delighted to announce my candidacy in Surrey Docks, representing Build. Southwark has a lot of the same challenges as the rest of Britain: ever rising housing costs, sky high energy bills, rapidly increasing petty crime and deteriorating public services.

Whilst these problems cannot all be fully solved at the local level, there are numerous steps we can take;

•Use S106 agreements innovatively to ensure the council has the ability to build its own council housing and is accountable to voters rather than relying on taking slices of private developments,  
•Encourage commonhold as opposed to leasehold developments and push central government to allow councils to preference commonhold when granting planning permission,
•Push back against the increasing statutory duties placed on councils by central government without a commensurate increase in central funding (one of the reasons why council tax bills continue to rise),
•Fight transport cuts in the borough, including the 14% reduction in the 381 bus service, 
•Demand the reversal of police cuts and stronger enforcement against phone snatchers and shoplifters.

Together we can build a better Southwark.

Yours sincerely,

Alex Baynham
